meta.tagline: I build AI products people can actually trust.
meta.subtagline: CSE grad and ex-Sandvine engineer, now an aspiring AI PM at IIM Kozhikode. I evaluate models, ship 0 to 1, and care most about outcomes — not output.

Represented my school basketball team; I still hoop, plus badminton and football.
My brother is doing his MBA at IIM Calcutta while I'm at IIM Kozhikode — a friendly Holy Trinity rivalry.
Big Telugu cinema buff and an anime watcher across genres.
Core committee at Rotaract Club, BMSCE — led a bunch of goodwill events.
Interned at Fidelity and Samsung R&D before my Sandvine engineering stint.
I funneled ~220 small language models down to the one that actually fit.
I declined a PPO to go all-in on becoming an AI PM at a frontier lab.
meta.quote: Strong opinions, loosely held — show me a sound argument and I'll change my mind fast.
aiQuotes: “The best way to predict the future is to build it.”

about.heading: About me
about.body: Hi, I'm Arun. I'm a CSE grad (BMSCE '22) who spent ~2 years as a software engineer at Sandvine, with earlier internships at Fidelity Investments and Samsung R&D. A layoff turned into a gap year — I used it to crack CAT and land at IIM Kozhikode. At my Wipro Consulting internship I evaluated ~220 small language models down to one and shipped a 0 to 1 BRD. Then I made the deliberate bet: I declined a pre-placement offer to chase becoming an AI Product Manager at a frontier lab. I think like an engineer, decide like a PM, and obsess over outcomes. I'm an ambivert — shy in new rooms, all-in once I'm comfortable — a critical thinker who'll happily change his mind for a better argument. Off the clock: anime, Telugu cinema, basketball, and trying new things.

projects.title: SLM evaluation & guardrails
projects.summary: Funneled ~220 small language models down to the single best fit for a client use case, with a guardrails layer for safe outputs.
projects.details: During my Wipro Consulting internship I owned the evaluation that took a field of ~220 small language models down to the one that actually fit the client's constraints. I built a scoring rubric across capability, latency, cost, and safety, ran structured comparisons, and designed a guardrails layer to keep outputs on-policy. The real lesson: picking a model is a product decision in disguise — the 'best' benchmark score loses to the model that fits the user, the budget, and the risk tolerance.

projects.title: Audience Brand Match
projects.summary: Took a new product from a blank page to a complete BRD — problem, users, requirements, and a path to v1.
projects.details: A greenfield concept to match audiences with the right brands. I led discovery, framed the core problem and target users, and translated it into a complete Business Requirements Document — scope, user stories, success metrics, and a prioritized path to a first version. It sharpened how I cut a fuzzy idea into something a team can actually build.

projects.title: Electives & attendance tracker
projects.summary: A small app I vibe-coded to fix a real pain at IIM K — tracking electives and attendance without the spreadsheet chaos.
projects.details: Every term at B-school, elective bidding and attendance turn into spreadsheet chaos. I scratched my own itch and built a small tracker app — scoping the problem, designing the flow, and shipping it fast with AI-assisted coding. Being a CSE grad who can prototype means I can pressure-test an idea myself before asking anyone to build it.

decks.title: SLM evaluation — how I picked 1 of 220
decks.description: My evaluation framework, scoring rubric, and guardrail thinking, walked through end to end.

decks.title: Product teardown — a 30-minute lens

thoughts.title: Why I declined a PPO to chase AI PM
thoughts.excerpt: The safe path was right there — a pre-placement offer in hand. Here's the reasoning behind walking away from it to bet on the role I actually want.

thoughts.title: What evaluating 220 models taught me about product judgment
thoughts.excerpt: Picking a model is a product decision in disguise. Benchmarks are the start, not the answer. Here's the lens I used to choose.

thoughts.title: Learning AI in public: QLoRA & beyond
thoughts.excerpt: I have working knowledge but real gaps in deep AI topics. Here's how I'm closing them — what's clicking, what isn't yet, and why I share the messy middle.

contact.heading: Let's build something
contact.body: I'm looking for AI Product Manager roles and love talking products, models, and the hard problems in between. If you're hiring or just want to swap notes, reach out.
